context:
Repo: /workspaces/jobtree, a Kubernetes GPU gang scheduler.

THE ONE FACT: an open v1.Lease CHARGES a budget and HOLDS GPUs. pkg/funding.Evaluate derives every funding class (Owned/Shared/Borrowed/Unfunded) from the set of OPEN leases + budgets + clock; the class is never stored. So a lease nobody closes bills forever and holds capacity forever, SILENTLY. Nothing crashes, nothing turns red. Funding class is per-LEASE, not per-run: one run can hold an Owned lease and an Unfunded lease at once.

The scheduler plugin (cmd/scheduler/plugin) is the SOLE COMMITTER: it mints exactly one Lease per pod at PreBind. controllers.CloseLease is the SOLE CLOSER, enforced by hack/antifake/soleclose.go with a ZERO allowlist. Three planes: ledger (Leases) / workload (Pods) / topology (Nodes). Bridge.apply deletes exactly the pods absent from state.Pods, and creates/updates exactly what is present — so forgetting one plane is silent.

UNDER REVIEW: the whole branch. `git diff $(git merge-base main HEAD)..HEAD` — 47 files, ~8000 insertions. Read it. Also `git log --oneline $(git merge-base main HEAD)..HEAD`. The load-bearing commits, newest first:

  c74e0ef feat(R27c) controllers/settle.go — a production sweep, wired into Bridge.WithWorld
  4e832a7 feat(R27b) controllers/quiescence_test.go — a randomized driver; TWO NEW INVARIANTS
  e681a96 fix(R28b) placement-group identity reaches the ledger and the pods
  9f6a744 fix(F4/F5/F7) releaseRun: a terminal run releases BOTH planes
  e2b5355 fix(#36/#39) the fencing verdict is re-taken UNDER the bridge lock
  8a1dc2c fix(R28a) one leaseGroupIndex; the reclaim was inert in production
  b4f6a6d fix(R27g/R27h) reclaimSquatter joins the phase tracker; width is total, not base gang
  7a681c0 fix(R27) pkg/invariant — the oracle, hooked into every engine entry point

READ FIRST, IN FULL: docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md (nine defect classes, each with the tell, where it lives, how to confirm it, and the specimen), AGENTS.md, and docs/project/history-run-phase-writers.md. They are the map, not background.

--- WHAT IS NEW IN THIS BRANCH, STATED PRECISELY (verify each claim; do not trust it) ---

(1) pkg/invariant — an ORACLE the test author did not write, hooked DEFERRED into all three engine entry points (RunController.Reconcile, ActivateReservations, HandleNodeFailure) via controllers/invariants.go:snapshotWorld(). Under `go test` a violation PANICS (invariant.UnderTest() checks os.Args[0] for `.test` — no opt-in). In production it logs and increments jobtree_invariant_violations_total. JOBTREE_INVARIANT=warn downgrades to warn for surveys. Invariants: INV-CLOSE-STAMPED, INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT, INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED, INV-GROUP-STAMPED, INV-CLOSED-MONOTONE, and TWO ADDED THIS BRANCH: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS (a terminal run has no pods) and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD (a run holding an open lease holds at least one pod).

FOUR invariants were considered and REJECTED because a LEGAL engine state refutes each; they are listed in pkg/invariant/invariant.go's package doc. An invariant that is wrong is not a weaker net, it is a REAPER. The two new ones have not been through a review.

(2) controllers/settle.go SettleLeases(state, now) — closes every open lease whose run is terminal (rule `terminal-run`) or whose Run object is absent (rule `orphan-run`), and DROPS THAT RUN'S PODS with it. Called from Bridge.WithWorld AFTER fn and BEFORE apply, under b.mu. Bridge.reportSweep: metrics.IncSweptLease(rule) + a Warning event; and if invariant.UnderTest() && sweep.Shirked() is non-empty, it calls invariant.Report (which PANICS) — a terminal-run closure is a TEST FAILURE, an orphan-run closure is not.

Fable designed a SECOND sweep rule, "an open Spare with no open Active of its group is an orphan". It was REFUTED and deliberately not shipped: a leftover spare-only run is a legal state (controllers/run_controller.go, the `allocated == 0` branch), and the plugin mints per pod so a spare legitimately exists before its actives. TestSweepLeavesASpareOnlyRunAlone is the executable refutation.

(3) controllers/quiescence_test.go — a generator over 800 enumerated seeds x 45 steps, 2 owners / 3 nodes / <=4 runs. It applies only LEGAL external events (node deleted, budget tightened, kubelet succeeds a pod, the plugin mints) and asserts the oracle after every step and at the settled fixpoint. mintPending() stands in for the plugin's PreBind mint.

(4) controllers/ledger_mirror_test.go mirrorPods() — INV-LEASE-HAS-POD found TWELVE existing test fixtures holding an open lease and no pods, a state the sole committer cannot produce. mirrorPods derives one pod per open lease and was inserted into all twelve. Each of those twelve tests pins a path that DELETES PODS and previously ran that half of the code against an empty slice.

(5) completeRun() now calls releaseRun() instead of carrying its own copy of the close-and-cull loop.

(6) reclaimSquatter(lease, victimKey, now, phases, ev) returns false => the caller must DECLINE the swap. It evicts at NODE granularity (nodesOfSlots), FAILS CLOSED if any doomed sibling lease of the victim on those nodes is not funding.ClassUnfunded, and calls removeRunPodsOnNodes. `ev := c.evaluate(now)` is computed ONCE before pass 2 — a documented semantic choice, not an optimisation.

(7) NodeReconciler.fenced(ctx, name) is called TWICE: a cheap filter outside the lock (decides nothing), and the VERDICT inside Bridge.WithWorld's closure. nodeFailed takes no clock and is true iff the Node is deleted or carries the out-of-service taint. NotReady and cordon are NEVER failures (R21: a cordoned node's pod keeps running; closing its lease and swapping produced TWO LIVE COPIES of one training rank).

--- SURROUNDING FACTS TO VERIFY, NOT ASSUME ---
- `make verify` is the gate and CI runs exactly it. `go test ./...` SILENTLY SKIPS the whole controllers/kube envtest suite when KUBEBUILDER_ASSETS is unset and still prints `ok`. `go test` without -v discards the stdout of PASSING tests, which hides JOBTREE_INVARIANT=warn output.
- The engine is PURE: a ClusterState plus a static clock IS a simulator. Any hypothesis about engine behaviour is a few minutes of work. DO NOT SPECULATE WHEN YOU CAN EXECUTE. Write the Go test, run it, paste the output.
- The whole suite, `make verify`, and 800 generator seeds are currently GREEN. That is exactly the condition under which every defect in the playbook shipped.
- Mutation testing: to check whether a test is decorative, COPY the file first (cp x.go /tmp/x.bak), revert the load-bearing line, confirm red, restore from the copy. NEVER `git checkout -- <file>`.

Not valid reasons to dismiss a finding: the suite passes; a comment says it cannot happen; it needs an unusual sequence (this scheduler runs for months — node failures, cordons, budget rollovers and restarts are routine); it is pre-existing (making a dead path reachable is worsening it, and a change that fails to achieve its stated purpose is a finding in its own right).

lenses.name: oracle-reaper
lenses.prompt: TASK: the two invariants added on this branch have never been reviewed. An invariant that is FALSE IN A LEGAL STATE is not a weaker safety net — it is a reaper: in production it fires a false alarm on healthy work, and in CI it turns a correct engine red and pressures the next author to 'fix' the engine to match. Four earlier candidates were killed exactly this way; their refutations are in pkg/invariant/invariant.go's package doc. Kill these two if you can. Do not argue from the doc comments — build the state.
questions: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD says: a run holding >=1 open lease holds >=1 pod. Find a LEGAL engine state that refutes it. Consider at least: a pod evicted or deleted out from under an open lease (kubelet eviction, node drain, `kubectl delete pod`); the moment between emitSwapPod deleting a consumed spare pod and the plugin minting the swap lease; removeSparePodOnNodes; a Run whose pods all reached Succeeded but whose leases Reconcile has not yet closed; growRun and shrinkRun; adoption after a controller restart. For each, say whether state.Pods is empty for that run at the RETURN of an engine entry point. Build the state in a Go test and run it.

INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS says: a terminal (Failed/Completed) run has zero pods. Enumerate EVERY writer of run.Status.Phase = Failed or Completed in the tree (docs/project/history-run-phase-writers.md counts twenty phase writers). For each, prove the pods are dropped on the same path, or find one that is not. Pay attention to failGroupWithoutSpare, the checkpoint-grace expiry in Reconcile, applyResolution's terminal branch, and anything in controllers/kube.

The oracle's projection lives in controllers/invariants.go:snapshotWorld(). It now computes Pods per run by counting EVERY pod of EVERY role, and KnownToLedger changed from `anyLease || anyPod` to `anyLease || podsOfRun[key] > 0`. Are those the same? Does counting SPARE pods toward Run.Pods make INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS or INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fire on a legal state that the previous projection tolerated?

AwaitingMint = activePods[key] > openActiveLeases[key] gates INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED. mirrorPods() adds exactly one pod per open lease. Does that make AwaitingMint FALSE in fixtures where it was previously true — and if so, has mirrorPods silently ENABLED a width check that those twelve tests were never written to satisfy, or silently DISABLED one? Which twelve tests, and what changed for each?

INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is checked for terminal runs too (the loop runs it before the `if r.Terminal { continue }`? read the code and say exactly). Is a terminal run with open leases and no pods reported once or twice, and does the ordering of the checks in CheckSteady change which violation an author sees first?
Take the single best refutation you found. Write the Go test, run it, and paste the real output. If you cannot refute either invariant, say so plainly and name the strongest legal state you tried.

lenses.name: sweep-safety
lenses.prompt: TASK: controllers/settle.go closes leases and deletes pods on EVERY Bridge.WithWorld pass, in a scheduler that runs for months. It is the newest, least-exercised code that can destroy funded work. Find the state in which it destroys something it should not — or in which it fails to close a lease it claims to close. Assume the author missed a race.
questions: SettleLeases derives `doomed` from state.Runs (terminal) and from leases+pods whose run key is ABSENT from state.Runs. Bridge.load lists Runs with APIReader (uncached). Enumerate every way state.Runs can be missing a Run that actually exists: a namespace filter, a List error swallowed, a Run created between the List and the Lease List, pagination, a Run in a namespace the manager does not watch. If any exists, the sweep closes a LIVE run's leases and deletes its pods. Trace Bridge.load line by line and cite it.

The plugin mints a Lease at PreBind, concurrently with the controller process. Construct the interleaving: pod bound -> plugin mints lease -> controller's WithWorld loads a world in which the Run is Failed (or absent) -> sweep closes the fresh lease and deletes the pod. Is that correct, or does it kill a pod the plugin has just funded? Now the reverse: the sweep closes a lease and drops the pod from state.Pods, apply deletes the pod, and the plugin mints a lease for it a moment later at PreBind. What closes THAT lease? Is it immortal?

reportSweep calls invariant.Report — which PANICS under `go test` — for rule=terminal-run. controllers/kube runs envtest with -race and real reconcilers. Find any envtest or unit test path where a Run legitimately becomes terminal while a lease is still open at the NEXT WithWorld (not the same one), which would make this panic a flake rather than a finding. Note that Reconcile's own deferred oracle would have panicked first — would it? Prove the two checks cannot disagree.

Sweep.Pods drops pods by `keys.NamespacedKey(pod.Namespace, pod.Labels[LabelRunName])`. What happens to a pod whose run-name label is empty or missing? Bridge.load filters with client.HasLabels{binder.LabelRunName} — does HasLabels match a label present but EMPTY? Check the client-go semantics, and say what key such a pod produces and whether it lands in `doomed`.

The sweep runs on EVERY WithWorld, including the pod reconciler, the node reconciler and the budget reconciler. Bridge.WithWorld returns fnErr AFTER apply. If fn returns an error mid-admission, the engine's half-applied state is applied AND then swept. Can a half-applied admission look terminal-or-orphan to the sweep? Build it.

cleanupDeletedRun already closes a deleted run's leases and sweeps its pods. SettleLeases' orphan-run rule now does the same thing. Two implementations of one obligation is playbook class 7. Do they agree on the ClosureReason, on the pods, and on the events? Which one wins when both run, and does the second one re-close (INV-CLOSED-MONOTONE) or skip?
Write the test for the single most dangerous case you found. Run it. Paste the output. controllers/kube/settle_bridge_test.go shows how to build a Bridge over a fake client with no envtest.

lenses.name: generator-honesty
lenses.prompt: TASK: controllers/quiescence_test.go is a generator whose whole value is reaching states nobody wrote a test for. A generator that runs green because it never reaches anything is worse than no generator: it is a green light nobody earned. Its first version generated no spares and no placement groups and passed in 0.25s. Prove the current one is not still decorative. MEASURE, do not reason.
questions: Instrument the driver (a scratch _test.go you then delete; never `git checkout`) and report, across all 800 seeds, how many times it reaches: a run in phase Running / Failed / Completed; a swap (lease closure reason `Swap`); `ReclaimedBySpare`; `SwapDeclined`; a spare lease; a run with two placement groups; an elastic grow; an elastic shrink; a resolver cut; a checkpoint-grace park; a reservation activation. Report the raw table. Name every branch of HandleNodeFailure, applyResolution and reconcileElasticRun that the generator NEVER reaches.

Mutation-test the generator against real, historical defects. For each of the following, revert it (copy the file first), run ONLY `go test -run Quiescence ./controllers`, and report whether the generator goes red and at which seed: (a) releaseRun drops its removeAllRunPods call; (b) reclaimSquatter calls removeAllRunPods(victim) instead of removeRunPodsOnNodes(victimKey, nodes); (c) controllers/invariants.go computes RunnableGPUs with baseGangGPUsForRun instead of runnableGPUsForRun; (d) leaseGroupIndex defaults a missing label to "0"; (e) reclaimSquatter's fail-closed loop (the `!classified || class != funding.ClassUnfunded` return false) is deleted; (f) runPhaseTracker's severity lattice is replaced by a plain last-write. Which of the six does the generator catch? Which are caught only by hand-written tests? Which are caught by NOTHING? That last set is the answer that matters.

The driver's settle() gives up after 12 rounds and merely LOGS `did not reach a fixpoint`. Count how many of the 800 seeds hit that cap. A history that never quiesces is either a livelock in the engine (a finding) or a generator that keeps changing the world (a bug in the driver). Which is it? If any seed hits the cap, name it and show the last three rounds' diffs.

mintPending() claims to mint 'exactly what cmd/scheduler/plugin does'. Diff it against cmd/scheduler/plugin/plugin.go's PreBind, line by line. Name every difference: the lease NAME (PreBind appends the run nonce from AnnotationRunNonce; mintPending does not), the payer segment, the node, gpusPerPod, the reason, the role, the group. For each difference, say what class of defect the driver is therefore blind to. The nonce one is not cosmetic: it is the ABA hazard from R2.

mirrorPods() was inserted into twelve existing tests. For each, run the test with mirrorPods REMOVED and report whether it still passes. A test that passes both with and without the pods it was just given is a test that does not look at the pod plane — say which of the twelve those are. Then check the converse: did adding pods change any assertion's meaning (for instance, does a swap now delete a pod the test used to leave alone, or does runGangComplete now see an active pod that is not Succeeded and refuse to complete a run the test expects to complete)?

The driver asserts pkg/invariant and NOTHING ELSE — no outcome assertions at all. Name three specific defect classes from the playbook that this design can never catch, and say for each whether an existing hand-written test covers it.
